chapter_info.subject: Science
chapter_info.chapter_name: Adolescence: A Stage of Growth and Change

points: Adolescence is the period of transition from childhood to adulthood, generally occurring between the ages of 10 and 19 years, marked by rapid physical, emotional, and behavioural changes.

Puberty is the stage during which the body undergoes observable and internal changes to become capable of reproduction; secondary sexual characteristics (e.g., voice change in boys, breast development in girls, hair growth in both) mark its onset.
The timing and extent of these changes vary among individuals — all such variations are completely normal.

Physical changes during adolescence include increase in height and weight, changes in body structure, voice change (Adam's apple in boys), growth of hair in armpits and pubic region, and appearance of acne due to increased oily skin secretions.

In girls, an important internal change is the onset of the menstrual cycle (every 28–30 days); the phase of blood discharge is called menstruation (lasts 3–7 days) and begins at puberty, ending naturally at age 45–55. Menstruation is a natural process and all myths around it have no scientific basis.
All changes during adolescence — physical and internal — are primarily regulated by hormones, chemical messengers produced in the body in response to signals from the brain.
Adolescence brings emotional changes such as mood swings, self-exploration, and heightened sensitivity; understanding these emotions helps adolescents make thoughtful and responsible choices.
A balanced diet (proteins, carbohydrates, calcium, iron, vitamins), regular personal hygiene, and daily physical activity are essential for healthy growth during adolescence.
Adolescents must avoid harmful and addictive substances (tobacco, alcohol, drugs) by firmly saying 'NO'; they should also use social media responsibly and be aware of cyberbullying.
